Common.siteDescription: Essential browser-based tools for productivity. No servers. Full privacy.

Home.subtitle: No servers. Full privacy. Everything runs in your browser.

faq.title: FAQ
faq.a1: BrowseryTools is a collection of free, open-source, browser-based utilities for images, files, text, audio/video and more—no sign up, no servers.
faq.q2: Do my files leave my device?
faq.a2: No. All processing happens locally in your browser using Web APIs like WebAssembly and Web Workers. Your files are not uploaded to a server.
faq.a3: Yes. All tools are free to use with no limits. If you find them useful, consider sharing the site and buying me a coffee.
faq.q4: Can I request a new tool?
faq.a4_pre: Absolutely. You can request or upvote ideas on our
faq.a5: Many tools continue to work after the initial load, but full offline support depends on your browser caching. We're improving this over time.
faq.a6_pre: Yes. BrowseryTools is fully open source. You can view the code, report issues, and contribute on our

HttpStatusCodes.title: HTTP Status Codes
HttpStatusCodes.subtitle: Complete reference for HTTP response status codes
HttpStatusCodes.searchPlaceholder: Search by code number, name, or keyword...
HttpStatusCodes.noResultsHint: Try a different search term or category

100.description: The client should continue with the request. Used to tell the client that the initial part of the request has been received.
100.useCase: Server acknowledges partial request; client should proceed.

101.description: Server is switching to a different protocol as requested by the client.

102.description: Server has received and is processing the request, but no response is available yet.
102.useCase: Preventing timeout on long-running WebDAV requests.

103.description: Used to return some response headers before final HTTP message.
103.useCase: Preloading resources before the full response is ready.

200.description: The request was successful. The meaning depends on the HTTP method used.
200.useCase: Standard response for successful GET, POST, PUT, DELETE.

201.description: The request succeeded and a new resource was created as a result.
201.useCase: POST request that successfully created a new user or record.

202.description: Request received but not yet acted upon. Processing happens asynchronously.
202.useCase: Triggering a background job or async task via API.

203.description: Returned metadata is not from the origin server but from a local or third-party copy.
203.useCase: Proxy servers transforming or caching responses.

204.description: Request succeeded but there is no content to send in the response.
204.useCase: DELETE or PUT that does not need to return data.

206.description: The server is delivering only part of the resource due to a range header sent by the client.
206.useCase: Video streaming or resumable file downloads.

207.description: Multiple status codes for multiple sub-requests in WebDAV.
207.useCase: WebDAV PROPFIND responses with multiple resource statuses.

208.description: Members of a DAV binding already enumerated in a previous reply.
208.useCase: WebDAV responses avoiding repetition of members.

226.description: Server fulfilled a GET request and the response is a result of instance manipulations.

301.description: The URL of the requested resource has changed permanently. The new URL is given in the response.
301.useCase: SEO-friendly permanent redirect from old URL to new URL.

302.description: The URL of the requested resource has changed temporarily. Further changes may occur.
302.useCase: Temporary redirect while a page is being updated.

303.description: Server sends this response to direct the client to get the requested resource at another URI with a GET request.
303.useCase: Redirecting after a POST form submission.

304.description: Used for caching. Tells the client that the response has not changed, so it can use its cached version.
304.useCase: Browser cache validation with ETag or Last-Modified headers.

307.description: Server sends this response to direct the client to get the requested resource at another URI with the same method.
307.useCase: Temporary redirect preserving the original HTTP method.

308.description: Permanent redirect that preserves the HTTP method used in the original request.
308.useCase: Permanent API endpoint migration preserving POST method.

400.description: Server cannot process the request due to malformed syntax or invalid request message framing.
400.useCase: Invalid JSON body or missing required query parameters.

401.description: Authentication is required and has not been provided or is invalid.
401.useCase: API request without a valid token or session.

402.description: Reserved for future use; sometimes used for digital payment requirements.
402.useCase: Paywall or subscription required to access content.

403.description: Client does not have access rights to the content. Unlike 401, the identity is known.
403.useCase: Authenticated user trying to access admin-only resource.

404.description: The server cannot find the requested resource. The URL is not recognized.
404.useCase: Accessing a page or resource that does not exist.

405.description: The request method is known but is not supported for the target resource.
405.useCase: Sending DELETE to an endpoint that only supports GET.

406.description: Server cannot produce a response matching the list of acceptable values defined in the request headers.
406.useCase: API returning only JSON but client asks for XML.

408.description: Server did not receive a complete request message within the time it was prepared to wait.
408.useCase: Client takes too long to send the request body.

409.description: The request conflicts with the current state of the server.
409.useCase: Trying to create a username that already exists.

410.description: The target resource is no longer available and will not be available again.
410.useCase: Permanently deleted content with no forwarding address.

411.description: Server refused the request because the Content-Length header field is missing.

413.description: Server refuses the request because the payload is larger than the server is willing to process.
413.useCase: File upload exceeding the maximum allowed size.

414.description: The URI requested by the client is longer than the server is willing to interpret.
414.useCase: Extremely long query strings in GET requests.

415.description: Server refuses to accept the request because the payload format is in an unsupported format.
415.useCase: Sending XML to an API endpoint that only accepts JSON.

422.description: Server understands the content type but is unable to process the instructions.
422.useCase: Form data with valid JSON but invalid field values.

423.description: The resource being accessed is locked.
423.useCase: WebDAV resource locked by another user.

425.description: Indicates the server is unwilling to risk processing a request that might be replayed.

429.description: User has sent too many requests in a given amount of time (rate limiting).
429.useCase: API rate limit exceeded; client should back off.

431.description: Server refuses to process the request because the header fields are too large.
431.useCase: Cookies or custom headers exceeding server limits.

451.description: Server is denying access to a resource due to legal demands.
451.useCase: Content blocked due to DMCA takedown or legal order.

500.description: Server has encountered a situation it does not know how to handle.

501.description: Request method is not supported by the server and cannot be handled.
501.useCase: Server does not support PATCH method on a resource.

502.description: Server, acting as a gateway, got an invalid response from upstream server.
502.useCase: Nginx receiving a bad response from the backend app server.

503.description: Server is not ready to handle the request. Common causes are down for maintenance or overloaded.
503.useCase: Server under maintenance or experiencing high traffic.

504.description: Server acting as a gateway did not get a timely response from the upstream server.
504.useCase: Upstream API taking too long to respond.

505.description: HTTP version used in the request is not supported by the server.
505.useCase: Client using HTTP/3 with a server that only supports HTTP/1.1.

506.description: Server has an internal configuration error in transparent content negotiation.
506.useCase: Misconfigured content negotiation on the server.

507.description: Server is unable to store the representation needed to complete the request.
507.useCase: WebDAV server running out of storage space.

508.description: Server detected an infinite loop while processing the request.

510.description: Further extensions to the request are required for the server to fulfil it.
510.useCase: Client needs to send an HTTP Extension Framework request.

511.description: Client needs to authenticate to gain network access.
511.useCase: Captive portal requiring login before accessing the internet.

ColorBlindnessSimulator.deuteranopiaDetail: Deuteranopia is a form of red-green color blindness where the eye lacks functioning green cone cells. Affects ~1% of males. Red and green hues are difficult to distinguish.
ColorBlindnessSimulator.protanopiaDetail: Protanopia is a type of red-green color blindness caused by the absence of red cone cells. Reds appear dark and dull. Affects ~1% of males.
ColorBlindnessSimulator.tritanopiaDetail: Tritanopia is a rare form of color blindness affecting blue-yellow perception, caused by missing blue cone cells. Blues appear green and yellows appear violet. Affects ~0.01% of people.
ColorBlindnessSimulator.achromatopsiaDetail: Achromatopsia (monochromacy) is the inability to see any color at all. The world appears in shades of gray. A very rare condition affecting roughly 1 in 30,000 people.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.intro: BrowseryTools is built so your files never have a reason to leave your device. This page explains, in plain terms, what that means and what little data we do collect.
privacy.noAccountsBody: There's nothing to register for. We don't ask for your name, email, or payment details, and we don't create a profile for you anywhere.
privacy.onDeviceHeading: Your files never leave your device
privacy.onDeviceBody: Every tool runs entirely in your browser. Images, documents, and text you process are read, transformed, and downloaded locally — nothing is uploaded to a server, ever. You can confirm this yourself: open your browser's network tab while using any tool.
privacy.analyticsBody: We use Vercel Analytics to see which pages and tools are popular. It reports anonymized, aggregate numbers — page views and rough usage counts — never the content of what you process, and never anything tied to you individually.
privacy.localStorageBody: Some tools remember your settings — like a theme or a recent list — using your browser's local storage. That data is saved on your device only and is never transmitted anywhere.
privacy.openSourceBody: The entire codebase is public on GitHub. If you want to know exactly what a tool does with your data, you can read the source yourself instead of taking our word for it.
privacy.contactBody: If anything here is unclear, the fastest way to reach us is GitHub.
privacy.changesBody: If this policy ever changes, we'll update this page. Since we don't collect emails, we have no way to notify you directly — check back if you're curious.

terms.title: Terms of Use
terms.intro: BrowseryTools is free software. By using it, here's what you're agreeing to — and it's shorter than most.
terms.licenseBody: The code is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License v3.0 (AGPL-3.0). You're free to read it, fork it, and run your own copy, under the terms of that license.
terms.freeBody: Every tool is free to use, for personal and commercial projects alike. No paywalls, no usage limits, no premium tier hiding the good parts.
terms.warrantyBody: BrowseryTools is provided as-is, with no warranty of any kind. We work to keep every tool accurate and reliable, but you're responsible for verifying results before relying on them for anything important.
terms.acceptableUseBody: Use the tools for lawful purposes. You're responsible for the content you process and for complying with any laws that apply to it.
terms.changesBody: We may update these terms as the project evolves. Continuing to use BrowseryTools after a change means you accept the new terms.
terms.contactBody: Questions about these terms are welcome — reach us on GitHub.
